items.passage: The human immune system defends the body against a constant assault of viruses, bacteria, and other foreign particles. White blood cells circulate through the bloodstream, scanning tissues for cells that look suspicious or damaged. When a threat is identified, specialized cells called lymphocytes produce antibodies that bind to the invader and mark it for destruction. Memory cells then preserve the recipe, allowing the body to respond much faster if the same intruder returns. This adaptive process is the foundation of modern vaccination.

Air pollution remains one of the most pressing environmental problems facing modern cities. Industrial smokestacks, vehicle exhaust, and the burning of household fuels release fine particles into the surrounding atmosphere. Once airborne, these particles can travel hundreds of kilometers before settling on soil, water, or human lungs. Long-term exposure is linked to higher rates of respiratory disease and contributes to the formation of acid rain. Recent regulations have curbed emissions in many regions, although enforcement still varies widely across countries.

Children acquire their first language with remarkable speed and consistency across very different cultures. By their second birthday, most toddlers already understand hundreds of words and string short phrases together. Researchers have proposed that a sensitive period in early childhood makes the brain especially receptive to grammatical patterns. Exposure to rich conversation appears to matter more than direct instruction or correction. After roughly age twelve, however, the ease with which new languages can be mastered begins to fade noticeably.

The electric telegraph dramatically transformed how distant communities exchanged news and trade information. Samuel Morse demonstrated his coded signals along a Baltimore-to-Washington line in 1844, and within twenty years cables spanned much of North America and Europe. Operators tapped messages letter by letter onto trembling brass keys, while clerks at the receiving station copied the incoming clicks onto paper slips. Important reports that had once required weeks now arrived in minutes. Newspapers, stock exchanges, and military commanders eventually depended on the technology completely.

Rocks are continually being created, transformed, and destroyed in a slow cycle that shapes the planet's surface. Molten material rising from deep within the Earth cools to form igneous rocks like basalt and granite. Wind, water, and ice eventually break these stones into sediments that settle in layers and harden into sedimentary rocks. Heat and pressure beneath the surface can further alter both types into metamorphic varieties such as marble or slate. The cycle has continued for billions of years.

Comets are small, icy bodies that travel along stretched, looping paths around the Sun. Most originate in two distant reservoirs: the Kuiper Belt beyond Neptune and the much more remote Oort Cloud. When a comet passes close to the Sun, surface ice vaporizes and forms a glowing coma surrounded by long tails of dust and gas. Astronomers have learned that this material preserves chemistry from the Solar System's earliest days. Some comets return on predictable schedules, while others appear only once.
